title: Why can glass take over a million years to decompose?
excerpt: Glass does not biodegrade, and its durable mineral network can weather so slowly that no single decomposition clock fits every environment.
shortAnswer: Glass can persist for an extremely long time because it does not biodegrade and its silica-rich structure weathers slowly, although no universal million-year timer exists.
choices: Its stable mineral network weathers extremely slowly
Its surface is too smooth for water to wear away
Microbes cannot live on its smooth surface
correctAnswer: Its stable mineral network weathers extremely slowly
correctFeedback: Exactly. Glass can break quickly, but changing the material itself can take an extraordinarily long time.
incorrectFeedback: Good guess. Glass persists mainly because its stable mineral network changes very slowly.
coolFact: Ancient glass can survive for thousands of years while its surface develops colorful, flaky layers through slow chemical reactions.
tryItYourself: Compare a clean glass jar with photos of sea glass. List the changes caused by tumbling and weathering, then notice that the material is still recognizably glass.
takeaway: Glass can shatter into fragments long before its material chemically changes, and its true lifespan depends on where it lands.
factReviewNotes: Verify how environmental agencies and scientific sources frame million-year persistence estimates.
Distinguish breaking, weathering, dissolving, and biodegrading throughout all user-facing copy.
Confirm the description of corrosion layers on ancient glass.
explanation:
Drop a glass bottle and it may disappear as a bottle in one sharp crash. Yet the glass itself has not decomposed. It has become smaller pieces, and those pieces can remain for an extraordinarily long time.

Most everyday glass begins with silica, a major ingredient in sand, combined with other minerals and melted together. After cooling, its atoms form a tightly connected, disorderly network. That structure is durable and does not provide the organic food that decomposer organisms can digest.

Glass is not unchangeable. Water and chemicals can slowly react with its surface. Temperature changes, waves, and impacts can crack it or grind it into smaller fragments. Different kinds of glass also weather at different rates in soil, seawater, or other environments.

That is why claims that glass takes a precise number of years to "decompose" should be treated cautiously. A figure such as one million years is a rough way to describe extreme persistence, not a measured countdown that applies everywhere. Scientists can observe ancient glass weathering, but nobody can assign every discarded bottle the same expiration date.

Glass is wonderfully reusable and recyclable because it is durable. That same durability becomes a problem when it is discarded: breaking the object is easy, but making the material truly disappear is not.
